title: Beef stew with herb dumplings
subtitle: Slow-braised chuck, root vegetables, suet dumplings.
excerpt: Beef chuck braised for two hours in dark stock until it breaks apart, set with root vegetables and topped with suet dumplings that steam in the stew for the final twenty minutes. The Sunday winter pot.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — beef stew and suet dumpling sections. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg (the braising and suet boiling chapters). Traditional British suet-dumpling technique documented across nineteenth and twentieth-century domestic cookery.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the stew without the dumplings for up to three months. Make fresh dumplings on the day of serving and add them directly to the reheated stew for the last 20 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Scales well. Double the stew in a larger casserole; make dumplings in proportion.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The stew is better made a day ahead — the fat rises and can be removed from the surface once cold, and the flavour deepens overnight. Reheat gently and add fresh dumplings to finish.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven for the long braise

content.text: Beef chuck is the right cut for a long braise: it has enough connective tissue to break down into a gelatinous, thick sauce over two hours, but enough muscle flavour to stand up to the vegetables and stock. The meat should be cut into large chunks (around 4 cm) and browned hard before any liquid goes in. The browning is not about colour for its own sake; it builds the savoury depth that carries the stew.

Suet dumplings go in for the last twenty minutes, steamed in the stew with the lid on. They should be soft and light inside, with a slight toughness to the outer surface that has been in contact with the liquid. If you find suet hard to source, a mix of frozen grated butter and plain flour produces a dumpling that is lighter but less rich — a different thing, but a good one.

attrs.title: On suet
attrs.body: Shredded beef or vegetable suet is sold in boxes in British supermarkets. Vegetable suet works identically in dumplings and is the right choice for those avoiding beef fat. The ratio is 2 parts self-raising flour to 1 part suet by weight — 150 g flour to 75 g suet here. If suet is unavailable, grate 75 g of cold unsalted butter and freeze for 15 minutes before mixing with the flour.

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Season {{beef-chuck}} of beef chuck chunks with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Dust lightly with {{plain-flour}} of plain flour. Heat {{rapeseed-oil}} of rapeseed oil in a heavy casserole over high heat. Brown the beef in batches (two or three chunks at a time, with room between them) for 3 to 4 minutes per side. Crowding the pan steams the meat instead of browning it. Remove browned batches and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{onion}} of roughly chopped onions to the casserole and cook for five minutes, scraping up the brown bits from the base. Add {{garlic}} of finely chopped garlic, {{carrot}} of carrot rounds, {{parsnip}} of parsnip chunks, and {{celery}} of celery chunks. Cook for three minutes. Stir in {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for two more minutes.

Return the beef to the pan. Pour in {{stock-beef}} of hot beef stock and add {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, the {{thyme}} of thyme sprigs, and the {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves. The stock should come up to just below the surface of the meat. Bring to the boil on the hob, then cover with the lid and transfer to the oven. Braise for 2 hours until the beef is completely tender and yields to gentle pressure.

About 25 minutes before serving, mix {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour with 75 g of shredded suet, the {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley, the fresh thyme leaves, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt. Add cold water (roughly 90 ml) tablespoon by tablespoon, stirring with a fork until the mixture just comes together into a soft, shaggy dough. Don't overwork it. Divide into eight equal portions and roll lightly between damp palms into rough balls.
Steam the dumplings in the stew

Remove the lid from the casserole and discard the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. The stew should be simmering gently. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Set the dumplings on top of the stew. They'll float. Replace the lid and return to the oven or keep on a low hob simmer for 20 minutes. The dumplings should be puffed and set through — a skewer goes in and comes out clean. Serve in deep bowls with the dumplings on top.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: The stew is forgiving; the dumplings are the only variable that can go unexpectedly.

items.symptom: Beef still tough after two hours
items.cause: Collagen needs sustained low heat to break down; temperature too high causes the muscle fibres to tighten before the collagen dissolves.
items.fix: Check the oven runs at the stated temperature. Keep the lid on for the full two hours; the beef should be braising at a quiet simmer, not boiling hard.

items.symptom: Dumplings dense and doughy in the centre
items.cause: Too much flour added, or the dough overworked, or the lid left off during steaming.
items.fix: Mix until the dough just comes together. Keep the lid on for the full 20 minutes of steaming without lifting it.

items.cause: Too much stock, or lid left ajar.
items.fix: Set the casserole on the hob without the lid and simmer until it reduces to the right consistency. A tablespoon of the cooking liquid whisked with a teaspoon of cornflour works as a quick fix.

items.cause: Not enough liquid in the dough, or overcooked.
items.fix: The dough wants to be soft but cohesive. Check at 20 minutes; dumplings are done when a skewer comes clean.

content.text: Beef stew with suet dumplings is the British winter Sunday lunch when the weather is too bleak for a roast. Mrs Beeton devotes several pages to beef stews and suet puddings in the 1861 book, treating them as serious cookery rather than peasant food. The dumpling is the element that elevates the stew from a weeknight braise to something worth staying home for: it absorbs the braising liquid, swells into the shape of a small cloud, and carries the flavour of the stew into every mouthful. Serve with mashed potato or swede, and a glass of something dark.
